title: How I Herded Hearts Instead of Sheep
article: Moonshine introduces himself as a caring, loving nursing companion who tried to be a therapy dog but, while too scared to do so, still inspired elderly patients.
author: Karen

content.intro: Woof! My name is Moonshine. Mom is in the other room, and she's working as always. Since my mother is busy, I wanted to share my tale of how I put smiles on my mom's patients' faces and learned about senior care.

sections.heading: My Training Adventure (And How It Didn't Go As Planned)
paragraphs: It started about a year and a half ago when Mom took me to some training to be a service dog. However, I got scared and it didn't work out! That's when Mom had a brilliant idea.
She started taking me into the nursing home where she works. Not to brag or anything, but I was a hit.

sections.heading: Meeting My New Friends
paragraphs: Most people there were very nice. I got lots of love from the residents. They all wanted to hold me and give me treats. Mom always brings special soft training treats that are easy for seniors with arthritis to hold and gentle on my tummy. In turn, I made them smile.

There was one lady who asked Mom if I could live with her. She said she loved dogs, and I was so adorable she wanted to keep me in the nursing home! Mom explained to her that I had a brother and sister who needed me to come home but promised we would come back to visit.

sections.heading: Learning About Memory Challenges
paragraphs: Sometimes it was sad because some of the residents acted a little different and yelled a lot, and that scared me. Mom said it was because they had dementia or Alzheimer's and couldn't remember things.
I still don't know what those big names mean, but Mom reassured me and said we would get some more treats if I continued to go and make the residents smile.

sections.heading: Putting on a Show
paragraphs: Sometimes Mom would make me heel and do tricks for the residents. Mom uses a quiet training clicker and keeps treats in a special treat pouch that doesn't make noise during visits. They all clapped and wanted me to come back.
It felt so good to see their faces light up when I did my sit, stay and shake commands. Even the staff members would gather around to watch my little performances.

sections.heading: What I've Learned About Helping Seniors
paragraphs: Through my visits to the nursing home, I've discovered that seniors really need us dogs to visit them.
Touch matters: Many residents haven't petted a dog in years. When they stroke my fur, you can see the joy in their eyes.
Memories come alive: Residents often tell me about dogs they used to have. I help them remember happy times.
Laughter is medicine: When I do my tricks or just act silly, it brings out genuine smiles and laughter.
Companionship heals: Sometimes just sitting quietly with someone is exactly what they need.

sections.heading: The Not-So-Fun Part
paragraphs: We do have to behave and have the one thing I hate — shots! Yikes! But Mom says it's important to keep everyone safe and healthy. We also use special antibacterial wipes after visits, and I take calming supplements before vet visits to help with my nerves.

sections.heading: What's Coming Next
paragraphs: Gotta run — here comes Mom! In future posts, I'll give you more information about my experiences visiting the folks in the nursing home and tell you about other things that other dogs and cats are doing to help seniors.
I'll share stories about my animal friends who work in hospitals, assisted living facilities and with home health patients. There's so much we furry companions can do to make life better for seniors!

sections.heading: A Message for Seniors and Their Families
paragraphs: If you're a senior missing the companionship of a pet, ask your facility about pet therapy programs. Many nursing homes and assisted living communities welcome visits from trained therapy animals.

Family members can also inquire about bringing well-behaved family pets for visits. Essential items like a portable water bowl, sanitizing wipes, and a comfortable travel mat make visits easier and safer for everyone. It might be one of the best gifts you can give your loved one.
Bye for now! Love, Moonshine.
Stay tuned for more adventures from Moonshine's Corner, where we explore how pets and therapy animals make a difference in senior care.

title: Medicare Home Health: What Seniors Need to Know
article: Medicare home health services can provide essential support for seniors, including skilled nursing care, physical therapy, and home health aide services.
author: Karen

content.intro: Welcome to the Senior Policy Beat, where we break down the most important issues affecting older adults and their care.

sections.heading: To Kick off the Senior Policy Beat
paragraphs: A blog dedicated to helping seniors and their families navigate the complex world of healthcare policy. In this opening edition, I want to share what I've learned from over 30 years as a nurse in Medicare Home Health.

One of the most important topics that I have gained great ability in throughout my career is Medicare Home Health. This knowledge is crucial for seniors, and I want to share what I've learned from decades of hands-on experience.

sections.heading: The Historical Roots of Home Health
paragraphs: The idea of home health can be traced all the way back to the ancients, who often traveled to see their patients or were stationed at the king's house.
This tradition continued to evolve in the early 20th century when nurses took on the role of seeing patients at the urge of local physicians.
Then came the mid-1960s and the creation of Medicare through Social Security. This was a game-changer for home health care.

sections.heading: What Home Health Is Today
paragraphs: Home health today has taken its historical roots and expanded on them. It's where a nurse comes to your home and helps you with your medical issues.
You can also receive physical therapy at home, and occupational therapy can also make sure you are safe in your home.
You can also have a home health aide for bathing. They usually come a couple of times a week. They do not do housekeeping.

sections.heading: When Everything Changed: The 1990s to 2000
paragraphs: In the 1990s, I was able to make visits to people's homes without worrying about strict rules and regulations.
Then came a new regulation by the government called the Prospective Payment System (PPS). It was introduced and adopted in 2000. This completely changed how we could provide care.

sections.heading: What PPS Means for You
paragraphs: Due to PPS, you must now qualify for these services. It's a Medicare payment system where home health agencies receive a predetermined, fixed payment for a 60-day episode of care.
What is OASIS? It is the set of questions and the assessment that the nurse performs that decides what you can qualify to have Medicare pay for.

sections.heading: What a Real Home Health Visit Looks Like
paragraphs: A normal visit can include a complete assessment, physician coordination, substantial documentation, and significant travel.
The workload was very doable unless you lived outside city limits, where mileage and time demands were much higher.

sections.heading: How Government Changes Affected Care
paragraphs: After the 1990s, the government stepped in again due to abuse and fraud in the system. Today regulations are stricter on what can be done, affecting patients directly.

sections.heading: So How Does This Affect You?
paragraphs: Nurses and therapists have less time to spend with you due to reduced visit windows.
The regulations also say you must be homebound, which allows limited outings but restricts extended travel.

sections.heading: What to Do If You Need Help
paragraphs: Call your home health agency and speak with leadership if service issues are not resolved.
Review star ratings and return surveys to help improve agency quality.

sections.heading: My Final Thoughts
paragraphs: Medicare is a wonderful service, but it could be so much better. I hope this helps you understand Medicare Home Health from someone who has lived it every day.
The tradition of house calls continues today, but it takes patients, families, nurses, and lawmakers working together to make it work well.

references.citation: Visiting Nurse Service of New York. Our History: Lillian Wald and the Henry Street Settlement.
Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services. History of Medicare & Medicaid.
Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services. Home Health Prospective Payment System.

title: The Heart of Hospice Nursing: Compassion, Comfort, and Connection
article: A reflection on hospice nursing, presence, family support, and comfort-focused care.
author: Christie Stephan RN ODG

content.intro: There are so many things I could say about nursing, but one of the most rewarding—and humbling—experiences has been working in hospice home care.

sections.heading: The Work of Hospice Nursing
paragraphs: Too often, patients come to hospice care later than they should in their disease journey. It's heartbreaking, yet an honor to walk alongside them in this final chapter.
When I first assess a patient for hospice, I explain the hospice philosophy — comfort, dignity, and quality of life — and become their advocate.

sections.heading: Connection with Patients and Families
paragraphs: There is an instant intimacy that forms between patient and nurse. Moments of trust can happen quickly and profoundly.
As a hospice nurse, I pray with families, help them cope with impending loss, hold hands, listen to memories, and provide reassurance.

sections.heading: Comfort and Clinical Support
paragraphs: Managing pain is one of the most important aspects of hospice care.
In addition to medications and comfort measures, gentle therapies can help patients feel more supported.

sections.heading: Final Reflection
paragraphs: Every day in hospice care reminds me of the power of compassion, connection, and hope.
My goal is always to help patients and families find comfort and dignity in every moment.

NewsSection: What to do before you die
title: Before I Go: What I've Learned About Death, Faith, and Protecting My Family
article: Estate and end-of-life planning guidance on legal documents, Medicaid look-back risk, and organizing records.
author: Karen

content.intro: People go through life not even considering their death. I've realized I believe I will be with Jesus when I die, and that brings peace — but I also needed to put important things in order.

sections.heading: What You Need to Do Before You Die
paragraphs: Preparing for death is not only spiritual — it is also legal and practical. Planning protects family and reduces crisis decisions.
Many people work their entire lives hoping to leave property to children, but without planning those assets can be consumed by long-term care costs.

sections.heading: The Missouri Wake-Up Call
paragraphs: Missouri increased the Medicaid look-back period to seven years before qualifying for some nursing home assistance.
If finances are not protected in time, homes and property may be recovered to pay for care.

sections.heading: Essential Legal Documents
paragraphs: Four core documents are recommended: a will, a trust, durable power of attorney for finances, and healthcare power of attorney.
Having the right person named to carry out your wishes is critical in emergencies.

sections.heading: What Else to Organize
paragraphs: Keep insurance policies, banking/password information, Social Security/Medicare cards, and emergency contacts organized and accessible.
Use a secure, labeled filing system so family can find what they need quickly.

sections.heading: A Nurse's Perspective
paragraphs: Preparing for death is one of the most loving things you can do for your family.
Faith can provide peace about what comes next, and preparation provides peace for those you leave behind.

sections.heading: Don't Wait — Act Now
paragraphs: The look-back period means planning must happen early.
Your family's financial security depends on decisions you make today.

references.citation: Missouri Department of Social Services. Medicaid Estate Recovery Program.
Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services. Medicaid Estate Recovery.

title: A Nurse's Journey: Memory Lane
article: A personal memoir from a reluctant student to a lifelong nurse, with hard lessons and meaningful moments in patient care.
author: Christie

content.intro: I wasn't a very good student in high school, but I worked hard and eventually earned my place in nursing.

sections.heading: Early Career and Learning on the Job
paragraphs: In earlier years of practice, roles and staffing looked very different from today.
I learned patient care, time management, and confidence through direct bedside work and support from experienced staff.

sections.heading: The Elevator Incident
paragraphs: One memorable and frightening experience happened while transporting a discharged patient on an old elevator. It became a story none of us forgot.

sections.heading: A Different Era of Medicine
paragraphs: In earlier decades, we did not have many of today's electronic tools, yet teams still responded quickly and delivered care with what was available.
Those experiences built resilience and prepared me for formal nursing education.

sections.heading: Reflections on a Lifetime of Care
paragraphs: The path from reluctant student to dedicated caregiver was not smooth, but every challenge helped shape my nursing identity.
Healthcare has changed, but the heart of nursing remains the same: presence, advocacy, compassion, and care in vulnerable moments.

title: What is an End-of-Life Doula and What Role Do They Have in End of Life Care?
article: An End-of-Life Doula provides emotional, spiritual, and practical support for individuals and families facing end-of-life decisions.
author: Kimberly Dorisio

content.intro: As we move through life we plan for many events, but very few people plan for end of life until a crisis forces the conversation.

sections.heading: Why We Avoid End-of-Life Planning
paragraphs: People are often left in shock, denial, or disbelief after a serious diagnosis and then told to get their affairs in order.
Legal planning is important, but it is only one part of end-of-life preparation.

sections.heading: How an End-of-Life Doula Can Help
paragraphs: An End-of-Life Doula is a non-medical professional who supports people and families with emotional, spiritual, and practical guidance.
Planning early is key — do not wait until illness and stress make decisions harder.

sections.heading: Three Essential Questions to Start Planning
paragraphs: 1) What matters to you?
2) Have you had conversations about your wishes with loved ones?
3) Have you named a healthcare proxy to carry out your wishes?

sections.heading: Helpful Resource
paragraphs: The Conversation Project provides free tools to help families start and structure end-of-life conversations.

sections.heading: Coming Next
paragraphs: Future topics include care needs, defining too much or not enough care, and comparing care settings such as hospice, palliative care, and home versus facility-based options.

sections.heading: About the Author
paragraphs: Kimberly Dorisio is a certified End-of-Life Doula with a nursing background who supports individuals and families through end-of-life planning with compassion and clarity.

title: Voices in Care: Stories from the Heart
article: In years of nursing, compassion and practical support define patient-centered care and caregiver resilience.
author: Christie

content.intro: In my years as a nurse, I've witnessed countless stories of resilience, hope, and healing.

sections.heading: Care from the Heart
paragraphs: Each patient and family brings their own journey, and it's a privilege to support them through difficult transitions.
Whether it's providing comfort, guidance, or simply a listening ear, the heart of care is always about compassion.

sections.heading: Small Comforts Matter
paragraphs: For people recovering at home or managing chronic conditions, practical comfort tools can support well-being for both patients and caregivers.
